context: A list of plant parts is given below. Place these in Column A to match the description of each part in Column B. The first one is completed as an example. List: Stamens; Phloem; Petals; Xylem; Carpels; Stomata.

solution: Stamens
Petals
Xylem
Carpels
Stomata
Phloem

context: Give one scientific reason why each of the following practices is carried out on Irish farms.

parts.text: Sheep are culled from the flock.
solution: To maintain flock health
To improve breeding stock
To control the population

parts.text: Cows are ‘dried off’ two months before calving.
solution: To reduce stress on the cow
To prevent calving complications
To ensure better milk production

parts.text: Trees in a forest plantation are thinned after about twenty years.
solution: To improve light penetration
To increase tree growth
To prevent overcrowding

parts.text: Lime is spread on some soils.
solution: To improve soil pH
To enhance nutrient availability
To reduce soil acidity

parts.text: Great care is taken when agitating slurry.
solution: To prevent the release of harmful gases
To minimize environmental impact
To ensure efficient use of nutrients

context: The two most common methods of grass conservation used in Ireland are silage production and hay production.

parts.text: Name two grass species commonly used in silage or hay production. At what stage should the grass be cut for good quality silage? A long spell of dry weather is essential for making hay. What is the scientific reason for this? Give the name of a bacterial species needed to make good quality silage.
solution: Grass species commonly used in silage or hay production include Timothy, Perennial Ryegrass, and Lucerne.
The grass should be cut at the early heading stage for good quality silage.
A long spell of dry weather is essential for making hay because it allows the grass to dry thoroughly, which prevents mold and ensures the hay has a longer storage life.
The bacterial species needed to make good quality silage is Lactobacillus.

A long spell of dry weather is essential for making hay because it allows the grass to dry thoroughly, which prevents mold and ensures the hay has a longer storage life.
The bacterial species needed to make good quality silage is Lactobacillus.

solution: Timothy

Lucerne

solution: early heading stage

subparts.text: A long spell of dry weather is essential for making hay. What is the scientific reason for this?
solution: A long spell of dry weather is essential for making hay because it allows the grass to dry thoroughly, which prevents mold and ensures the hay has a longer storage life.

solution: Lactobacillus

parts.text: Describe the steps you would carry out to make good quality hay. Give three advantages of silage production over hay production. Give three advantages of round bale silage over pit silage. Describe a laboratory or field experiment to estimate percentage (%) dry matter in a silage sample.
solution: Steps to make good quality hay: 1. Cut the grass at the proper height. 2. Let the grass dry in the field. 3. Bales the hay. 4. Store the hay in a dry, well-ventilated area.
Advantages of silage production over hay production: 1. Silage can be made in a shorter period of time. 2. Silage has a longer storage life. 3. Silage has a higher feed value.
Advantages of round bale silage over pit silage: 1. Round bale silage is easier to store. 2. Round bale silage is easier to feed. 3. Round bale silage is less prone to spoilage.

Laboratory experiment to estimate percentage (%) dry matter in a silage sample: 1. Weigh a representative sample of silage. 2. Dry the sample in an oven at 105°C until constant weight is reached. 3. Weigh the dried sample. 4. Calculate the percentage (%) dry matter by dividing the weight of the dried sample by the weight of the original sample and multiplying by 100.

subparts.text: Describe the steps you would carry out to make good quality hay.
solution: 1. Cut the grass at the proper height.
2. Let the grass dry in the field.
3. Bales the hay.
4. Store the hay in a dry, well-ventilated area.

subparts.text: Describe a laboratory or field experiment to estimate percentage (%) dry matter in a silage sample.
solution: 1. Weigh a representative sample of silage.
2. Dry the sample in an oven at 105°C until constant weight is reached.
3. Weigh the dried sample.
4. Calculate the percentage (%) dry matter by dividing the weight of the dried sample by the weight of the original sample and multiplying by 100.

parts.text: Describe a laboratory or field experiment to estimate percentage (%) dry matter in a silage sample.
solution: 1. Weigh a representative sample of silage.
2. Dry the sample in an oven at 105°C until constant weight is reached.
3. Weigh the dried sample.
4. Calculate the percentage (%) dry matter by dividing the weight of the dried sample by the weight of the original sample and multiplying by 100.

context: Beef breeds and dairy breeds are commonly found on Irish cattle farms.

parts.text: Name two beef breeds and two dairy breeds. Describe three differences between the conformation of beef and dairy breeds.
solution: Beef breeds: Angus, Hereford. Dairy breeds: Holstein, Jersey.

Differences: Beef breeds tend to have a more muscular build and are larger, while dairy breeds have a more slender and elegant build. Dairy breeds produce more milk, while beef breeds are raised for meat production. Beef breeds have a higher fat content, while dairy breeds have a higher protein content.

subparts.text: Name two beef breeds and two dairy breeds.
solution: Beef breeds: Angus, Hereford. Dairy breeds: Holstein, Jersey.

subparts.text: Describe three differences between the conformation of beef and dairy breeds.
solution: Differences: Beef breeds tend to have a more muscular build and are larger, while dairy breeds have a more slender and elegant build. Dairy breeds produce more milk, while beef breeds are raised for meat production. Beef breeds have a higher fat content, while dairy breeds have a higher protein content.

parts.text: Condition scoring is an important skill in cattle farming. Explain what is meant by condition scoring in cattle. What condition score is recommended for cows at mating? Explain why a high condition score in cows can cause problems at calving.
solution: Condition scoring in cattle is a method used to evaluate the body condition of an animal based on a set of standardized criteria. It helps farmers assess the animal's health, nutrition, and overall fitness. A condition score of 3 is recommended for cows at mating. A high condition score in cows can cause problems at calving because it may indicate that the cow is overweight or obese, which can lead to difficulties during labor and increase the risk of complications.

A condition score of 3 means that the animal is in good condition and is neither too thin nor too fat. It indicates that the animal is well-nourished and has a healthy body fat percentage. A cow with a condition score of 3 is likely to have a good appetite, strong muscle development, and a well-defined rump.

A high condition score in cows can cause problems at calving because it may indicate that the cow is overweight or obese. This can lead to difficulties during labor, as the cow may not be able to easily give birth. A high condition score can also increase the risk of other complications during calving, such as dystocia or the need for forceps intervention. Therefore, it is important to maintain a healthy condition score for cows to ensure a successful calving process.

parts.text: State two precautions that should be taken when buying in replacement heifers. Describe two common practices aimed at keeping livestock diseases from entering a farm.
solution: Precautions when buying in replacement heifers: Inspect the heifers for any visible health issues, check their vaccination history, and ensure that they are in good body condition. Practices to keep livestock diseases from entering a farm: Maintain good hygiene, keep the farm clean and well-ventilated, and avoid introducing new animals to the farm.
